title: Valid vs. Same Padding
concept: "Valid" padding means no padding at all — the output shrinks. "Same" padding adds just enough border to keep the output the same size as the input.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does adding a 1-pixel zero border to a 5x5 image turn it into, before the filter slides over it?
exercises.explain: Right — a 1-pixel border on every side adds 2 total to each dimension: 5 + 1 + 1 = 7.

cards.title: Two padding philosophies
cards.body: So far you've seen padding exists — but how much padding should you actually add? CNNs mostly settle on one of two standard answers, and they have very different personalities.

cards.title: Valid vs. same padding
cards.body: Valid padding means adding zero padding — the filter only ever slides within the image's real pixels, so the output shrinks. Same padding means adding just enough zero border so the output ends up the exact same width and height as the input.

cards.title: Cropping a photo vs. keeping the full frame
cards.body: Valid padding is like cropping a photo to only the fully-in-focus center — you lose some border, but everything left is untouched original data. Same padding is like taping extra blank cardboard around the photo first, so the final printed frame keeps the exact same size as the original.

cards.title: Same numbers, two outcomes
cards.body: A 5x5 image, 3x3 filter, stride 1. With valid padding (no border added), the filter only fits at 3x3 = 9 positions, so the output is 3x3 — smaller than the input. With same padding, a 1-pixel zero border is added first (5x5 becomes 7x7), and now the filter fits at 5x5 = 25 positions — an output that's 5x5, matching the original input exactly.

cards.title: "Valid" doesn't mean "correct"
cards.body: The name "valid" makes it sound like the better or more correct choice. It just means the filter is only considered valid when it's fully inside the real pixels — no padding trickery. "Same" isn't a hack either; it's simply padded enough to preserve size.

cards.title: Valid vs. Same, side by side
cards.body: Same 5x5 input, same 3x3 filter, two padding choices:

left.text:
No border added
5x5 input → 3x3 output
Shrinks every layer

right.text:
Just enough border added
5x5 input → 5x5 output
Size preserved

exercises.prompt: A 5x5 image runs through a 3x3 filter with 'valid' padding and stride 1. What size is the output?

options.text: 3x3 — smaller than the input, since no border was added

exercises.explain: Right — with no padding at all, the filter only fits at 3x3 = 9 positions, giving a 3x3 output.

exercises.prompt: True or false: 'Same' padding means the output is always exactly the same width and height as the input.
exercises.explain: True — that's the entire point of same padding: add just enough border so the output size matches the input size.

exercises.sentence: ___ padding adds zero pixels around the border, so the output size stays equal to the input size.
exercises.explain: Same padding is named for exactly this outcome — it keeps the output the same size as the input.

options.text: No padding is added, so the filter only ever sits fully inside the original pixels and the output shrinks
Extra zero padding is added so the output always grows larger than the input
The image is resized to a fixed square before the filter runs

exercises.explain: Valid padding is the 'no shortcuts' option: zero extra border, filter only considered valid where it's entirely inside the real image.

exercises.prompt: You're stacking 8 convolution layers to build a deep feature extractor, and you want the spatial size to stay constant so you can keep stacking layers without the image shrinking away to nothing. Which padding choice fits, and why?

options.text: Same padding — it adds just enough border at each layer to keep the output size equal to the input size, layer after layer
Valid padding — because it always keeps the output the same size
Same padding — because it always makes the output larger than the input
Valid padding — because it's simply the 'correct' choice by name

exercises.explain: Exactly. Same padding is built for exactly this situation — stacking many layers while keeping the spatial size constant. Next up: seeing stride and padding's effects on output size side by side.
